file update:
diff --git a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
index 0dff7689fbb17df46f540503da793adb82031298..d7ddabe8011b59b9e3005d691f29e13a3babb4c5
--- a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
+++ b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
@@ -171,7 +171,7 @@
 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.
 \end{example}
 
-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
 
 \section{Bridgeland stability conditions and wall-crossing}
 \label{sec:bridgeland-stab}
@@ -183,7 +183,7 @@
 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

almost an isomorphism, and $\Phi_{10}$ (the unique genus-$2$
cusp form of weight~$10$) is directly accessible. The

\begin{conjecture}[DDCA--toroidal bridge; \ClaimStatusConjectured]
\label{conj:ddca-toroidal-bridge}
 succeeded in 52ms:
\section{Five routes to $G(K3 \times E)$}
\label{sec:k3e-five-routes}

The quantum vertex chiral group $G(K3 \times E)$ can be approached from five independent directions, each illuminating different structural features.

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Route \arabic*.}, leftmargin=*]
 \item \textbf{BLLPRR (Bryan--Leung--Lian--Pandharipande--Ruan--R).} Start from the DT theory of $K3 \times E$ (Theorem~\ref{thm:dt-igusa}). The DT partition function $Z^X = C / \Delta_5^2$ gives $\mathfrak{g}_{\Delta_5}$ via the product formula (Theorem~\ref{thm:k3e-product}). The CoHA (Theorem~\ref{thm:k3e-coha}) gives $U(\fn_+)$. The braiding comes from the MO $R$-matrix (Theorem~\ref{thm:k3e-mo-rmatrix}). \emph{Status}: proved (modulo standard conjectures on DT/GW for $K3 \times E$).

diff --git a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
index 0dff7689fbb17df46f540503da793adb82031298..d7ddabe8011b59b9e3005d691f29e13a3babb4c5

 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.
 \end{example}
 
-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.

 \end{definition}

**Considering line wrapping issues**

I think the line wrap might be causing some issues, particularly with the `\textup` line breaks that look the same. I wonder if I could patch each occurrence with smaller, exact one-liners to fix it. Maybe I should use one patch for each occurrence and pay attention to surrounding lines. I’m also considering whether there could be a carriage return involved. Okay, let’s try patching one by one to see if that helps!

diff --git a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
index 0dff7689fbb17df46f540503da793adb82031298..d7ddabe8011b59b9e3005d691f29e13a3babb4c5
--- a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
+++ b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
@@ -171,7 +171,7 @@
 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.
 \end{example}
 
-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
 
 \section{Bridgeland stability conditions and wall-crossing}
 \label{sec:bridgeland-stab}
@@ -183,7 +183,7 @@
 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

-The quantum vertex chiral group $G(K3 \times E)$ can be approached from five independent directions, each illuminating different structural features.
+The quantum vertex chiral group $G(K3 \times E)$ admits five comparison routes.
 
 \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Route \arabic*.}, leftmargin=*]
  \item \textbf{BLLPRR (Bryan--Leung--Lian--Pandharipande--Ruan--R).} Start from the DT theory of $K3 \times E$ (Theorem~\ref{thm:dt-igusa}). The DT partition function $Z^X = C / \Delta_5^2$ gives $\mathfrak{g}_{\Delta_5}$ via the product formula (Theorem~\ref{thm:k3e-product}). The CoHA (Theorem~\ref{thm:k3e-coha}) gives $U(\fn_+)$. The braiding comes from the MO $R$-matrix (Theorem~\ref{thm:k3e-mo-rmatrix}). \emph{Status}: proved (modulo standard conjectures on DT/GW for $K3 \times E$).
@@ -5796,7 +5796,7 @@
 complementary limits: the DDCA by specializing one spectral variable

-conjecture identifies the precise bridge between them.
+conjecture states the bridge between them.

 \label{conj:ddca-toroidal-bridge}

The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.
 \end{example}
 
-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
 
 \section{Bridgeland stability conditions and wall-crossing}
 \label{sec:bridgeland-stab}
@@ -183,7 +183,7 @@
 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

diff --git a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
index 0dff7689fbb17df46f540503da793adb82031298..d7ddabe8011b59b9e3005d691f29e13a3babb4c5
--- a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
+++ b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
@@ -171,7 +171,7 @@
 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.
 \end{example}
 
-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
 
 \section{Bridgeland stability conditions and wall-crossing}
 \label{sec:bridgeland-stab}
@@ -183,7 +183,7 @@
 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

+The additional structure---distinguishing the Mukai lattice

 in the \emph{full root-lattice-graded} bar Euler product,
 which records root multiplicities at each lattice vector.

@@ -5,5 +5,5 @@
 
-What happens is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
+The operative mechanism is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
 
-The answer is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces a richer pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
+The governing input is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces the relevant pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
 
@@ -541,3 +541,3 @@
 \noindent\textbf{Part~2: Verdier dual.}
-The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). The key point is that $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
+The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). Thus $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.

 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

+The additional structure---distinguishing the Mukai lattice

diff --git a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
index f85e600ca46f765bfa7052a2cffa58fcc8d39da8..451cd4827448c498e95874300cd3576d00d3016f
--- a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
+++ b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
@@ -3,9 +3,9 @@
 
 Factorization algebras on a complex curve carry an $\Etwo$ structure; factorization algebras on a point carry an $\Eone$ structure. Where is the transition? The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} gives a definite answer for low dimensions: $E_\infty$ at $d = 1$, $\Etwo$ at $d = 2$, $\Eone$ at $d = 3$. The pattern suggests the formula $E_{4-d}$, but this formula breaks at $d = 4$, because $E_0$ does not exist as a separate operad (it is the category of pointed objects). Something else must happen.
 
-What happens is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
+The operative mechanism is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
 
-The answer is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces a richer pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
+The governing input is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces the relevant pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
 
 
 \section{Dunn additivity and the $E_n$ hierarchy}
@@ -539,7 +539,7 @@
 
 \medskip
 \noindent\textbf{Part~2: Verdier dual.}
-The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). The key point is that $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
+The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). Thus $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
 
 The cohomological isomorphism $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y)) \cong H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!))$ as graded vector spaces follows from both sides being exterior algebras on $3$ generators with the same Poincar\'{e} polynomial $(1+t)^3$.

diff --git a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
index 0dff7689fbb17df46f540503da793adb82031298..d7ddabe8011b59b9e3005d691f29e13a3babb4c5
--- a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
+++ b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
@@ -171,7 +171,7 @@
 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.
 \end{example}
 
-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.

diff --git a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
index f85e600ca46f765bfa7052a2cffa58fcc8d39da8..451cd4827448c498e95874300cd3576d00d3016f
--- a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
+++ b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
@@ -3,9 +3,9 @@
 
 Factorization algebras on a complex curve carry an $\Etwo$ structure; factorization algebras on a point carry an $\Eone$ structure. Where is the transition? The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} gives a definite answer for low dimensions: $E_\infty$ at $d = 1$, $\Etwo$ at $d = 2$, $\Eone$ at $d = 3$. The pattern suggests the formula $E_{4-d}$, but this formula breaks at $d = 4$, because $E_0$ does not exist as a separate operad (it is the category of pointed objects). Something else must happen.
 
-What happens is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
+The operative mechanism is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
 
-The answer is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces a richer pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
+The governing input is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces the relevant pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
 
 
 \section{Dunn additivity and the $E_n$ hierarchy}
@@ -539,7 +539,7 @@
 
 \medskip
 \noindent\textbf{Part~2: Verdier dual.}
-The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). The key point is that $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
+The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). Thus $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
 
 The cohomological isomorphism $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y)) \cong H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!))$ as graded vector spaces follows from both sides being exterior algebras on $3$ generators with the same Poincar\'{e} polynomial $(1+t)^3$.

diff --git a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
index 0dff7689fbb17df46f540503da793adb82031298..d7ddabe8011b59b9e3005d691f29e13a3babb4c5
--- a/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
+++ b/chapters/examples/derived_categories_cy.tex
@@ -171,7 +171,7 @@
 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.
 \end{example}
 
-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
 
 \section{Bridgeland stability conditions and wall-crossing}
 \label{sec:bridgeland-stab}
@@ -183,7 +183,7 @@
 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces the relevant pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
 
 
 \section{Dunn additivity and the $E_n$ hierarchy}
@@ -539,7 +539,7 @@
 
 \medskip
 \noindent\textbf{Part~2: Verdier dual.}
-The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). The key point is that $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
+The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). Thus $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
 
 The cohomological isomorphism $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y)) \cong H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!))$ as graded vector spaces follows from both sides being exterior algebras on $3$ generators with the same Poincar\'{e} polynomial $(1+t)^3$.

@@ -171,7 +171,7 @@
 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.
 \end{example}
 
-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
 
 \section{Bridgeland stability conditions and wall-crossing}
 \label{sec:bridgeland-stab}
@@ -183,7 +183,7 @@
 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

diff --git a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
index f85e600ca46f765bfa7052a2cffa58fcc8d39da8..451cd4827448c498e95874300cd3576d00d3016f
--- a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
+++ b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
@@ -3,9 +3,9 @@
 
 Factorization algebras on a complex curve carry an $\Etwo$ structure; factorization algebras on a point carry an $\Eone$ structure. Where is the transition? The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} gives a definite answer for low dimensions: $E_\infty$ at $d = 1$, $\Etwo$ at $d = 2$, $\Eone$ at $d = 3$. The pattern suggests the formula $E_{4-d}$, but this formula breaks at $d = 4$, because $E_0$ does not exist as a separate operad (it is the category of pointed objects). Something else must happen.
 
-What happens is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
+The operative mechanism is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
 
-The answer is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces a richer pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
+The governing input is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces the relevant pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.

M /Users/raeez/calabi-yau-quantum-groups/chapters/theory/e1_chiral_algebras.tex
@@ -130,3 +130,3 @@
 \label{rem:three-bars-vol3}
-The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} has three natural composites. $\Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C)$; this is the ordered bar sensed by the Yangian. $\mathrm{av} \circ \Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\Sigma}(A_C)$; this is the symmetric bar sensed by the modular characteristic $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$. The full $E_2$ enhancement $\Phi_{E_2}$ produces the Drinfeld-centered bar $B^{E_2}(A_C) = \cZ(B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C))$; this is the braided bar sensed by the quantum group. All three factor through $\Phi_{E_1}$. This is the volume's motto: the ordered bar is the primitive.
+The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} has three natural composites. $\Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C)$; this is the ordered bar sensed by the Yangian. $\mathrm{av} \circ \Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\Sigma}(A_C)$; this is the symmetric bar sensed by the modular characteristic $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$. The full $E_2$ enhancement $\Phi_{E_2}$ produces the Drinfeld-centered bar $B^{E_2}(A_C) = \cZ(B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C))$; this is the braided bar sensed by the quantum group. All three factor through $\Phi_{E_1}$. In this volume, the ordered bar is the primitive object.
 \end{remark}
@@ -148,3 +148,3 @@
 
-For $\cC = D^b(\Coh(K3))$ the output is the positive half of the BKM superalgebra studied in \ref{part:examples}: the Borcherds denominator arises as the Euler character of $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_{K3})$, and $\kappa_{\mathrm{BKM}} = 5$ is distinct from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}} = 3$.
+For $\cC = D^b(\Coh(K3))$, the resulting K3 chiral algebra has $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}} = 2$. The Borcherds-side weight $\kappa_{\mathrm{BKM}} = 5$ belongs to the $K3 \times E$ lift developed in Part~\ref{part:examples} and must not be conflated with the K3 value.
 
@@ -171,3 +171,3 @@
 \label{rem:convolution-linfty-e1}
-For an $E_1$-chiral algebra $A$ and a coalgebra $C$, the convolution $L_\infty$-algebra $\hom_\alpha(C, A)$ of Volume~I is the natural habitat of Maurer-Cartan elements controlling twisted morphisms. On the $E_1$ side, $\hom_\alpha$ is a strict dg Lie algebra when the ordering of $C$ is preserved (the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$), and a genuine $L_\infty$-algebra when higher brackets are included ($\Conv_\infty$). The MC moduli of the two models coincide, but the full $L_\infty$-structure is required for homotopy transfer, formality statements, and gauge equivalence. For Vol~III purposes, the key point is that the CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ lands in the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$ because the cyclic $A_\infty$ structure on $A_\cC$ is strict at degree two; higher-degree corrections require the $L_\infty$ refinement.
+For an $E_1$-chiral algebra $A$ and a coalgebra $C$, the convolution $L_\infty$-algebra $\hom_\alpha(C, A)$ of Volume~I is the natural habitat of Maurer-Cartan elements controlling twisted morphisms. On the $E_1$ side, $\hom_\alpha$ is a strict dg Lie algebra when the ordering of $C$ is preserved (the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$), and a genuine $L_\infty$-algebra when higher brackets are included ($\Conv_\infty$). The MC moduli of the two models coincide, but the full $L_\infty$-structure is required for homotopy transfer, formality statements, and gauge equivalence. For Vol~III, the CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ lands in the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$ because the cyclic $A_\infty$ structure on $A_\cC$ is strict at degree two; higher-degree corrections require the $L_\infty$ refinement.
 \end{remark}

 Affine space $X = \C^3$, viewed as the local CY$_3$ total space over a point, has Beilinson quiver the Jordan quiver with three loops and cubic superpotential $W = \mathrm{tr}(XYZ - XZY)$. The critical CoHA is $Y^+(\widehat{\fgl}_1)$ (Schiffmann--Vasserot 2013). The full affine Yangian is $\cW_{1+\infty}$ at the self-dual level (Proch\'azka--Rap\v{c}\'ak 2018), verifying the five-step functor chain of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}.

-Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
+Across all three examples the pattern is the same: Beilinson quiver $\to$ superpotential $\to$ critical CoHA $\to$ positive half of an affine (super) Yangian $\to$ $\Eone$-sector of the Vol~III chiral algebra, via the CY-to-chiral functor for toric CY$_3$ without compact $4$-cycles (Theorem~\ref{thm:rsyz}). The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
 
 \section{Bridgeland stability conditions and wall-crossing}
 \label{sec:bridgeland-stab}
@@ -183,7 +183,7 @@
 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

 \begin{remark}[Three bars, one functor]
 \label{rem:three-bars-vol3}
-The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} has three natural composites. $\Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C)$; this is the ordered bar sensed by the Yangian. $\mathrm{av} \circ \Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\Sigma}(A_C)$; this is the symmetric bar sensed by the modular characteristic $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$. The full $E_2$ enhancement $\Phi_{E_2}$ produces the Drinfeld-centered bar $B^{E_2}(A_C) = \cZ(B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C))$; this is the braided bar sensed by the quantum group. All three factor through $\Phi_{E_1}$. This is the volume's motto: the ordered bar is the primitive.
+The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} has three natural composites. $\Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C)$; this is the ordered bar sensed by the Yangian. $\mathrm{av} \circ \Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\Sigma}(A_C)$; this is the symmetric bar sensed by the modular characteristic $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$. The full $E_2$ enhancement $\Phi_{E_2}$ produces the Drinfeld-centered bar $B^{E_2}(A_C) = \cZ(B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C))$; this is the braided bar sensed by the quantum group. All three factor through $\Phi_{E_1}$. In this volume, the ordered bar is the primitive object.
 \end{remark}
 
 \section{$E_1$-chiral algebras from CY categories}
@@ -146,7 +146,7 @@
 The cyclic $\Ainf$-structure on $\cC$ provides an associative product on the bar coalgebra $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_\cC) = T^c(s^{-1}\bar{A}_\cC)$ via OPE residues (Step~1 of the cyclic-to-chiral passage, Section~\ref{sec:cyclic-to-chiral} of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral}). The holomorphic direction is the Hochschild differential $b \colon \mathrm{CC}_\bullet(\cC) \to \mathrm{CC}_{\bullet-1}(\cC)$, which descends to the bar differential $d_{\mathrm{bar}}$ on $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_\cC)$ via the standard identification of the bar construction with the Hochschild chain complex. The ordered direction is the $S^1$-action on the cyclic bar complex: the Connes $B$-operator cyclically permutes the bar entries, and its restriction to the ordered bar preserves the deconcatenation coproduct. The $E_1$-chiral structure is the factorization algebra on $C$ obtained by the factorization envelope of the Lie conformal algebra $\mathfrak{L}_\cC$ (Step~2), with the negative-cyclic trace in $\mathrm{HC}^-_2(\cC)$ providing the quantization datum (Step~4). At $d=2$, no framing obstruction arises: the $\bS^2$-framing is automatic (Kontsevich--Vlassopoulos). The full construction is Theorem~\ref{thm:cy-to-chiral} of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral}.
 \end{proof}

 \begin{remark}[Convolution $L_\infty$-algebra on the $E_1$ side]
 \label{rem:convolution-linfty-e1}
-For an $E_1$-chiral algebra $A$ and a coalgebra $C$, the convolution $L_\infty$-algebra $\hom_\alpha(C, A)$ of Volume~I is the natural habitat of Maurer-Cartan elements controlling twisted morphisms. On the $E_1$ side, $\hom_\alpha$ is a strict dg Lie algebra when the ordering of $C$ is preserved (the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$), and a genuine $L_\infty$-algebra when higher brackets are included ($\Conv_\infty$). The MC moduli of the two models coincide, but the full $L_\infty$-structure is required for homotopy transfer, formality statements, and gauge equivalence. For Vol~III purposes, the key point is that the CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ lands in the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$ because the cyclic $A_\infty$ structure on $A_\cC$ is strict at degree two; higher-degree corrections require the $L_\infty$ refinement.
+For an $E_1$-chiral algebra $A$ and a coalgebra $C$, the convolution $L_\infty$-algebra $\hom_\alpha(C, A)$ of Volume~I is the natural habitat of Maurer-Cartan elements controlling twisted morphisms. On the $E_1$ side, $\hom_\alpha$ is a strict dg Lie algebra when the ordering of $C$ is preserved (the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$), and a genuine $L_\infty$-algebra when higher brackets are included ($\Conv_\infty$). The MC moduli of the two models coincide, but the full $L_\infty$-structure is required for homotopy transfer, formality statements, and gauge equivalence. For Vol~III, the CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ lands in the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$ because the cyclic $A_\infty$ structure on $A_\cC$ is strict at degree two; higher-degree corrections require the $L_\infty$ refinement.
 \end{remark}
 
 \begin{remark}[Comparison with Beilinson-Drinfeld chiral algebras]
diff --git a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
index f85e600ca46f765bfa7052a2cffa58fcc8d39da8..451cd4827448c498e95874300cd3576d00d3016f
--- a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
+++ b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
@@ -3,9 +3,9 @@
 
 Factorization algebras on a complex curve carry an $\Etwo$ structure; factorization algebras on a point carry an $\Eone$ structure. Where is the transition? The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} gives a definite answer for low dimensions: $E_\infty$ at $d = 1$, $\Etwo$ at $d = 2$, $\Eone$ at $d = 3$. The pattern suggests the formula $E_{4-d}$, but this formula breaks at $d = 4$, because $E_0$ does not exist as a separate operad (it is the category of pointed objects). Something else must happen.
 
-What happens is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
+The operative mechanism is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
 
-The answer is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces a richer pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
+The governing input is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces the relevant pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
 
 
 \section{Dunn additivity and the $E_n$ hierarchy}
@@ -539,7 +539,7 @@
 
 \medskip
 \noindent\textbf{Part~2: Verdier dual.}
-The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). The key point is that $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
+The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). Thus $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
 
 The cohomological isomorphism $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y)) \cong H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!))$ as graded vector spaces follows from both sides being exterior algebras on $3$ generators with the same Poincar\'{e} polynomial $(1+t)^3$.

The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is the subject of Chapter~\ref{ch:toric-coha}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.

The passage from $\Eone$ to $\Etwo$ requires the Drinfeld center, and is developed in Chapter~\ref{ch:drinfeld-center}. In every case the modular characteristic is of type $\kappa_{\mathrm{cat}}$ (holomorphic Euler characteristic of the base Fano) and must be distinguished from $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$ (computed intrinsically from the resulting chiral algebra); agreement between the two is a prediction of the functor, verified at $d = 2$ and conjectural at $d = 3$.
 
 \section{Bridgeland stability conditions and wall-crossing}
 \label{sec:bridgeland-stab}
@@ -183,7 +183,7 @@
 A \emph{Bridgeland stability condition} on a triangulated category $\cC$ is a pair $\sigma = (Z, \cP)$, where $Z \colon K_0(\cC) \to \C$ is a group homomorphism (the \emph{central charge}) and $\cP = \{\cP(\phi)\}_{\phi \in \bR}$ is a slicing of $\cC$ into full additive subcategories (the \emph{semistable objects of phase $\phi$}), subject to: (i) for $0 \neq E \in \cP(\phi)$, $Z(E) = m(E) \exp(i \pi \phi)$ with $m(E) > 0$; (ii) $\cP(\phi + 1) = \cP(\phi)[1]$; (iii) Harder--Narasimhan filtrations exist (Bridgeland 2007).
 \end{definition}
 
-For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold has rich structure. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
+For CY$_3$ categories the stability manifold can have many wall-crossing chambers. On K3 surfaces, Bridgeland (2008) proved that $\Stab(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ is a connected, simply connected complex manifold containing the complexified K\"ahler cone as an open subset. The autoequivalence group $\mathrm{Aut}(D^b(\Coh(X)))$ acts on $\Stab(X)$ by deck transformations, and the quotient is a period domain.
 
 \begin{example}[Stability conditions on K3]
 \label{ex:stab-k3}

 \begin{remark}[Three bars, one functor]

-The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} has three natural composites. $\Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C)$; this is the ordered bar sensed by the Yangian. $\mathrm{av} \circ \Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\Sigma}(A_C)$; this is the symmetric bar sensed by the modular characteristic $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$. The full $E_2$ enhancement $\Phi_{E_2}$ produces the Drinfeld-centered bar $B^{E_2}(A_C) = \cZ(B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C))$; this is the braided bar sensed by the quantum group. All three factor through $\Phi_{E_1}$. This is the volume's motto: the ordered bar is the primitive.
+The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} has three natural composites. $\Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C)$; this is the ordered bar sensed by the Yangian. $\mathrm{av} \circ \Phi_{E_1}$ produces $B^{\Sigma}(A_C)$; this is the symmetric bar sensed by the modular characteristic $\kappa_{\mathrm{ch}}$. The full $E_2$ enhancement $\Phi_{E_2}$ produces the Drinfeld-centered bar $B^{E_2}(A_C) = \cZ(B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_C))$; this is the braided bar sensed by the quantum group. All three factor through $\Phi_{E_1}$. In this volume, the ordered bar is the primitive object.
 \end{remark}

 The cyclic $\Ainf$-structure on $\cC$ provides an associative product on the bar coalgebra $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_\cC) = T^c(s^{-1}\bar{A}_\cC)$ via OPE residues (Step~1 of the cyclic-to-chiral passage, Section~\ref{sec:cyclic-to-chiral} of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral}). The holomorphic direction is the Hochschild differential $b \colon \mathrm{CC}_\bullet(\cC) \to \mathrm{CC}_{\bullet-1}(\cC)$, which descends to the bar differential $d_{\mathrm{bar}}$ on $B^{\mathrm{ord}}(A_\cC)$ via the standard identification of the bar construction with the Hochschild chain complex.  \begin{remark}[Convolution $L_\infty$-algebra on the $E_1$ side]
 \label{rem:convolution-linfty-e1}
-For an $E_1$-chiral algebra $A$ and a coalgebra $C$, the convolution $L_\infty$-algebra $\hom_\alpha(C, A)$ of Volume~I is the natural habitat of Maurer-Cartan elements controlling twisted morphisms. On the $E_1$ side, $\hom_\alpha$ is a strict dg Lie algebra when the ordering of $C$ is preserved (the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$), and a genuine $L_\infty$-algebra when higher brackets are included ($\Conv_\infty$). The MC moduli of the two models coincide, but the full $L_\infty$-structure is required for homotopy transfer, formality statements, and gauge equivalence. For Vol~III purposes, the key point is that the CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ lands in the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$ because the cyclic $A_\infty$ structure on $A_\cC$ is strict at degree two; higher-degree corrections require the $L_\infty$ refinement.
+For an $E_1$-chiral algebra $A$ and a coalgebra $C$, the convolution $L_\infty$-algebra $\hom_\alpha(C, A)$ of Volume~I is the natural habitat of Maurer-Cartan elements controlling twisted morphisms. On the $E_1$ side, $\hom_\alpha$ is a strict dg Lie algebra when the ordering of $C$ is preserved (the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$), and a genuine $L_\infty$-algebra when higher brackets are included ($\Conv_\infty$). The MC moduli of the two models coincide, but the full $L_\infty$-structure is required for homotopy transfer, formality statements, and gauge equivalence. For Vol~III, the CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ lands in the strict model $\Conv_{\mathrm{str}}$ because the cyclic $A_\infty$ structure on $A_\cC$ is strict at degree two; higher-degree corrections require the $L_\infty$ refinement.
 \end{remark}

diff --git a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
index f85e600ca46f765bfa7052a2cffa58fcc8d39da8..451cd4827448c498e95874300cd3576d00d3016f
--- a/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
+++ b/chapters/theory/en_factorization.tex
@@ -3,9 +3,9 @@
 
 Factorization algebras on a complex curve carry an $\Etwo$ structure; factorization algebras on a point carry an $\Eone$ structure. Where is the transition? The CY-to-chiral functor $\Phi$ of Chapter~\ref{ch:cy-to-chiral} gives a definite answer for low dimensions: $E_\infty$ at $d = 1$, $\Etwo$ at $d = 2$, $\Eone$ at $d = 3$. The pattern suggests the formula $E_{4-d}$, but this formula breaks at $d = 4$, because $E_0$ does not exist as a separate operad (it is the category of pointed objects). Something else must happen.
 
-What happens is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
+The operative mechanism is stabilization. The $\bS^d$-framing on $\HH_\bullet(\cC)$ provides an $E_d$-algebra structure, but for $d \geq 3$ the restriction to the chiral level via Dunn additivity produces only an $\Eone$-algebra. The higher $E_d$-structure does not disappear: it survives as \emph{additional shifted symplectic and shifted Poisson data} beyond the base $\Eone$ level, classified by the framing obstruction. The question then becomes: for which CY dimensions $d$ is this shifted data nontrivial, and what invariant detects it?
 
-The answer is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces a richer pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
+The governing input is Bott periodicity. The framing obstruction lives in $\pi_d(BU)$ or $\pi_d(BO)$ or $\pi_d(B\Sp)$ depending on the parity and reduction of the structure group of the CY pairing. For the unitary path, $\pi_d(BU) = \Z$ when $d$ is even and vanishes when $d$ is odd. For the symplectic/orthogonal path, the 8-fold periodicity of the classical groups produces the relevant pattern, with refinements at $d \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. The main result of this chapter (Theorem~\ref{thm:e1-stabilization-cy}) assembles these obstruction computations into a single statement: the framing obstruction is trivial precisely when $d \bmod 8 \in \{1, 3, 7\}$, and the CY chiral algebra is $\Eone$-stabilized with additional shifted structure controlled by $\pi_d(BU)$ elsewhere.
 
 
 \section{Dunn additivity and the $E_n$ hierarchy}
@@ -539,7 +539,7 @@
 
 \medskip
 \noindent\textbf{Part~2: Verdier dual.}
-The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). The key point is that $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
+The Verdier duality $D_{\C^3}$ on conilpotent $E_3$-coalgebras inverts the Omega-background: $(h_1, h_2, h_3) \mapsto (-h_1, -h_2, -h_3)$. The structure function transforms as $g^!(u) = g(-u) = g(u)^{-1}$, which has the \emph{same} OPE pole structure (simple poles at $u = \pm h_i$, with residues of the same order). Thus $g^!(u)$ defines a class~$L$ algebra $Y^!$ (the shadow class is determined by the pole order, not the residue signs). The spectral sequence argument of Part~1 applies verbatim to $Y^!$, giving $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!)) \cong \Lambda^\bullet(\kk^3)$.
 
 The cohomological isomorphism $H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y)) \cong H^\bullet(B_{E_3}(Y^!))$ as graded vector spaces follows from both sides being exterior algebras on $3$ generators with the same Poincar\'{e} polynomial $(1+t)^3$.
